note: 2020年7月最近有很多关于特权的讨论。虽然这个概念被过度使用了，但它还是有一定的道理，特别是特权会让你失明的想法，让你无法看到那些生活与你截然不同的人所能看到的东西。但是，这类失明最普遍的例子之一，我从未见过明确提到过。我要称它为正统特权：一个人的思想越传统，在他们看来，每个人表达自己的观点就越安全。对他们来说，发表意见是安全的，因为他们的意见来源是目前可以接受的任何东西。因此，在他们看来，这对每个人来说都是安全的。从字面上讲，他们无法想象会给他们带来麻烦的真实说法。然而，在历史的每一个时刻，都有一些真实的事情会让你陷入可怕的麻烦之中。我们的是第一个不是这样的地方吗？那将是多么惊人的巧合啊。当然，这至少应该是默认的假设，即我们的时代并不是独一无二的，有一些真实的事情你现在不能说，就像过去一直存在的那样。你会认为。但是，即使面对如此压倒性的历史证据，大多数人还是会大胆地去做这件事。东正教特权的典型特征是，你为什么不直接说出来呢？如果你认为有些事情是真实的，人们不能说，为什么你不勇敢地拥有它呢？更极端的人甚至会指责你他们认为你心里一定有特定的异端邪说，尽管如果你那个时代有不止一种异端邪说，这些指控往往是不确定的：你必须要么是Bean Xist，要么是Yist。与这些人打交道固然令人沮丧，但重要的是要认识到他们是认真的。他们并不是假装认为一个想法既非正统又真实是不可能的。在他们看来，世界真的是这样的。你如何回应正统特权？仅仅给它起个名字可能会有一些帮助，因为当你遇到它的时候，它会提醒你，为什么和你交谈的人看起来如此不合理，因为这是一种独特的顽强的特权形式。人们可以通过了解更多他们不了解的东西来克服大多数形式的特权造成的盲目性。但是他们不能仅仅通过学习更多的知识来克服正统特权。他们必须变得更加独立。如果这真的发生了，那也不是在一次谈话的时间范围内发生的。也许可以让一些人相信正统特权是存在的，尽管他们感觉不到它，就像人们对暗物质可以感觉到的那样。例如，可能会有一些人相信，这是历史上第一个你不能说没有真实的东西的时刻，即使他们想象不出具体的例子，这也是非常不可能的。但是，除了这些人，我认为对这类特权说“检查一下你的特权”是行不通的，因为那些在这类特权中的人并没有意识到他们也在享受这项特权的权利。“。在思想保守的人看来，他们并不是思想传统。在他们看来，他们是对的。事实上，他们往往特别肯定这一点。也许解决办法就是诉诸礼貌。如果有人说他们能听到你听不到的高音噪音，那只是礼貌地相信他们的话，而不是要求他们拿出不可能拿出的证据，或者干脆否认他们听到了什么。想象一下这听起来有多粗鲁。同样，如果有人说他们能想到一些真实但不能说出来的事情，那么只有礼貌地相信他们的话，即使你自己想不到任何事情。一旦你意识到正统特权的存在，很多其他的事情就会变得更加清晰。例如，怎么会有一大批理性的聪明人担心他们所谓的取消文化，而另一些理性的聪明人却认为这是个问题呢？“。一旦你理解了正统特权的概念，就很容易看出这种分歧的根源。如果你相信没有什么是你不能说的，那么任何因为他们所说的事情而陷入麻烦的人都是罪有应得。感谢山姆·奥尔特曼、特雷弗·布莱克威尔、帕特里克·科里森、安东尼奥·加西亚-马丁内斯、杰西卡·利文斯顿、罗伯特·莫里斯、迈克尔·尼尔森、杰夫·拉尔斯顿、马克斯·罗瑟和哈吉·塔格阅读了这本书的草稿。
note_en: July 2020  There has been a lot of talk about privilege lately. Although theconcept is overused, there is something to it, and in particularto the idea that privilege makes you blind that you can't seethings that are visible to someone whose life is very differentfrom yours.  But one of the most pervasive examples of this kind of blindnessis one that I haven't seen mentioned explicitly. I'm going to callit  orthodox privilege: The more conventional-minded someone is, themore it seems to them that it's safe for everyone to express theiropinions.  It's safe for them to express their opinions, because the sourceof their opinions is whatever it's currently acceptable to believe.So it seems to them that it must be safe for everyone. They literallycan't imagine a true statement that would get them in trouble.  And yet at every point in history, there were true things that wouldget you in terrible trouble to say. Is ours the first where thisisn't so? What an amazing coincidence that would be.  Surely it should at least be the default assumption that our timeis not unique, and that there are true things you   can't say now,just as there have always been. You would think. But even in theface of such overwhelming historical evidence, most people will gowith their gut on this one.  The spectral signature of orthodox privilege is "Why don't you justsay it?" If you think there's something true that people can't say,why don't you be brave, and own it? The more extreme will evenaccuse you of specific heresies they imagine you must have in mind,though if there's more than one heresy current in your time, theseaccusations will tend to be nondeterministic: you must either bean xist or a yist.  Frustrating as it is to deal with these people, it's important torealize that they're in earnest. They're not pretending they thinkit's impossible for an idea to be both unorthodox and true. Theworld really looks that way to them.  How do you respond to orthodox privilege? Merely giving it a namemay help somewhat, because it will remind you, when you encounter it,why the people you're talking to seem so strangely unreasonable.Because this is a uniquely tenacious form of privilege. People canovercome the blindness induced by most forms of privilege by learningmore about whatever they're not. But they can't overcome orthodoxprivilege just by learning more. They'd have to become moreindependent-minded. If that happens at all, it doesn't happen onthe time scale of one conversation.  It may be possible to convince some people that orthodox privilegemust exist even though they can't sense it, just as one can with,say, dark matter. There may be some who could be convinced, forexample, that it's very unlikely that this is the first point inhistory at which there's nothing true you can't say, even if theycan't imagine specific examples.  But except with these people, I don't think it will work to say"check your privilege" about this type of privilege, because thosein its demographic don't realize they're in it. It doesn't seem toconventional-minded people that they're conventional-minded. Itjust seems to them that they're right. Indeed, they tend to beparticularly sure of it.  Perhaps the solution is to appeal to politeness. If someone saysthey can hear a high-pitched noise that you can't, it's only politeto take them at their word, instead of demanding evidence that'simpossible to produce, or simply denying that they hear anything.Imagine how rude that would seem. Similarly, if someone says theycan think of things that are true but that cannot be said, it'sonly polite to take them at their word, even if you can't think ofany yourself.  Once you realize that orthodox privilege exists, a lot of otherthings become clearer. For example, how can it be that a largenumber of reasonable, intelligent people worry about something theycall "cancel culture," while other reasonable, intelligent peopledeny that it's a problem? Once you understand the concept of orthodoxprivilege, it's easy to see the source of this disagreement. Ifyou believe there's nothing true that you can't say, then anyonewho gets in trouble for something they say must deserve it.             Thanks to Sam Altman, Trevor Blackwell, Patrick Collison, Antonio Garcia-Martinez,Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, Michael Nielsen , Geoff Ralston, Max Roser, andHarj Taggar for reading drafts of this.

面对一种致命的病毒，许多美国学院和大学选择通过大量或完全在线教授秋季课程来保护他们的社区。上周，联邦政府故意扰乱了这些计划，提出了一项新规定，如果他们的课程是远程授课的，今年秋天可能会有数十万外国学生无法在美国学习。
哈佛大学校长拉里·巴考(Larry Bacow)称这一举动是“残忍和鲁莽的”，这是一种迫使机构开放课程的策略，就像大流行已经消失一样。周二，在哈佛大学和我的机构麻省理工学院(Massachusetts Institute Of Technology)发起的联合诉讼以及来自其他许多方面的压力下，政府撤销了这项政策。
然而，更大的战斗远未结束。这一被误导的政策是政府希望外国学生远离的众多信号之一-这种态度反映了对我们国家利益的严重误解。
在任何长期的竞争中，没有人比你的竞争对手更了解你的长处。几年前，在我参加的一次晚宴上，中国科技领袖认为，中国最重要的经济优势是规模：中国庞大的人口和市场提供了永久性的支撑。但他们也评论了美国在科学创造力方面的持续优势。
是什么给了我们国家这样的优势？他们的解释使我大吃一惊。这些领导人告诉我，因为美国是多种多样的，它吸引了来自世界各地的最优秀和最聪明的人一起工作和创造。他们说，这对中国来说要困难得多。
这一敏锐的观察完美地捕捉到了为什么强迫外国学生放弃在这里学习对美国来说是灾难性的弄巧成拙：正是在经济竞争激烈的时候，我们正在系统性地削弱我们的竞争对手最羡慕的力量。
为什么外国人才对美国如此重要？出于同样的原因，波士顿红袜队并不局限于波士顿出生的球员：你抽出的池越大，优秀人才的供应量就越大。此外，通过培养国内顶尖学生和国际顶尖学生，美国获得了巨大的创新优势。通过相互挑战、激励和延伸，他们可以让彼此变得更好，就像明星球员提高整个球队的水平一样。
不幸的是，当你拒绝伟大的球员时，对手球队会很高兴地签下他们。其他国家正在努力吸引那些因为日益增长的反移民敌意或官僚障碍而对美国产生反感的学生。
作为一个国家，当我们背弃有才华的外国学生时，我们不仅失去了他们给我们的教室和实验室带来的所有东西，我们还放弃了一项战略资产。
首先，我们失去了建设这个国家的那种个人动力：才华横溢的年轻人的生命力，他们有勇气和雄心离开熟悉的一切，寻找更美好的未来。更重要的是，大多数来这里攻读博士学位的学生留下来是为了建立自己的家庭和职业生涯，而且往往是为了建立能够创造数千个就业机会的公司。许多人成为了公民。
例如，最新的数据显示，来自中国的博士生中，83%的人在完成学位五年后仍在美国，他们是那种训练有素的科学家和工程师，推动了美国的创新。
如果美国长期的政策不要求很多学生在完成教育后回国-这种制度就像培养一名伟大的球员，然后坚持让她去为竞争对手效力一样，这个比例会更高。最近，留在这里的博士毕业生比例开始下降，部分原因是我们的国家信息是，他们不受欢迎。
随着华盛顿的一些人试图限制外国学生，特别是来自中国的学生，这种敌意的信息变得更加响亮。
当然，美国必须对申请签证的学生进行筛选，并将那些背景可疑的学生拒之门外。但即使是最猛烈的对华鹰派人士也承认，当外国利益集团从事间谍活动或窃取知识产权时，他们会寻求招募资深科学家；只有少数中国学生卷入了这类案件。绝大多数我们应该欢迎，而不是因最近政策中明显的直截了当的敌意而泄气。
我深深地相信，我们必须增加接受科学和工程培训的美国人的数量。但我们也必须明白，美国在科学和工程方面的实力是美国实力的核心，几十年来，这种实力的一个核心要素就是我们吸引世界上最优秀人才的能力。
作为全世界人民的希望灯塔，这个国家获得了许多无形的优势；1974年，我第一次从委内瑞拉来到美国，我的父母最终在那里定居下来，成为希特勒统治下的欧洲的难民。我来这里是为了通过攻读研究生学位来改善自己的前景。但我发现了一种开放、大胆、独具匠心和任人唯贤的文化，这种文化告诉我，来到美国，我就真正回到了家。
我们的竞争对手公开羡慕我们欢迎和吸纳各地人才的能力。最近，我担心我们只有在美国失去这一战略力量后才会认识到这一点。
“泰晤士报”致力于向编辑发表各种信件。我们想听听您对这篇文章或我们的任何一篇文章的看法。这里有一些小贴士。这是我们的电子邮件：Letters@nytimes.com。
note_en:
Confronted with a deadly virus, many American colleges and universities are choosing to protect their communities by  teaching fall classes largely or entirely online. Last week, the federal government deliberately disrupted those plans by proposing a new rule that would have prevented potentially hundreds of thousands of foreign students from studying in the United States this fall if their classes were taught remotely.
 Harvard’s president, Larry Bacow, termed this move “cruel and reckless,” a ploy to force institutions to open classes as if the pandemic had vanished. On Tuesday, responding to a joint lawsuit led by Harvard and my institution, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, as well as pressure from many other quarters,  the government revoked the policy.
 Yet the larger battle is far from over. This misguided policy was one of many signals that the administration wants foreign students to stay away — an attitude that reflects a stark misreading of our national interest.
 In any long-running competition, no one understands your strengths better than your rivals. At a dinner I attended a few years back, Chinese tech leaders contended that China’s most important economic advantage is scale: China’s vast population and market offer a permanent leg up. But they also remarked on America’s persistent advantage in scientific creativity.
 What gives our country this advantage? Their explanation surprised me. Because the U.S. is heterogeneous, these leaders told me, it draws the best and brightest from all over the world to work and create together. This, they said, was much more difficult for China.
 This astute observation perfectly captures why forcing foreign students to abandon their studies here would be disastrously self-defeating for America: Precisely at a time of sharp economic rivalries, we are systematically undermining the very strength our competitors envy most.
 Why is foreign talent so important to the United States? For the same reason the Boston Red Sox don’t limit themselves to players born in Boston: The larger the pool you draw from, the larger the supply of exceptional talent. Moreover, America gains immense creative advantage by educating top domestic students alongside top international student s. By challenging, inspiring and stretching one another, they make one another better, just as star players raise a whole team’s level of play.
 Unfortunately, when you turn away great players, rival teams happily sign them. Other countries are working hard to attract students who have soured on the United States because of growing anti-immigrant hostility or bureaucratic roadblocks.
 As a nation, when we turn our backs on talented foreign students, we not only lose all that they bring to our classrooms and laboratories, we also give up a strategic asset.
 First, we lose the kind of personal drive that built this country: the life force of brilliant young people with the courage and ambition to leave everything familiar in search of a better future. What’s more, most students who come here to earn a Ph.D. stay to build their families and careers, and often companies that create thousands of jobs. Many become citizens.
 The latest data show, for example, that  83 percent of Ph.D. students from China, the kind of highly trained scientists and engineers who drive American innovation, were still in the United States five years after completing their degrees.
 The percentage would be higher if longstanding U.S. policies did not require many students to return home after finishing their education — a system as counterproductive as training a great player and then insisting that she go play for a rival team. Recently, the percentage of doctoral graduates remaining here has begun to decline, in part because our national message is that  they are not welcome.
 As some in Washington have sought to limit foreign students, especially those from China, that hostile message has grown louder.
 Of course the United States must screen students seeking visas and keep out those with dubious backgrounds. But even the fiercest China hawks acknowledge that when foreign interests engage in espionage or intellectual theft, they seek to recruit senior scientists; only a small number of Chinese students have been implicated in such cases. The vast majority we should welcome, not discourage with the blunt hostility apparent in recent policies.
 I believe profoundly that we must increase the number of Americans pursuing  training in science and engineering. But we must also understand that America’s strength in science and engineering is central to America’s strength, period — and that a core element of that strength, for decades, has been our ability to lure the world’s finest talent.
 This country derives many intangible advantages from being a beacon of hope for people around the world; I first came to America in 1974 from Venezuela, where my parents finally settled as refugees from Hitler’s Europe. I came to improve my own prospects through a graduate degree. But I found a culture of openness, boldness, ingenuity and meritocracy — a culture which taught me that in coming to America, I had truly come home.
 Our competitors openly envy our capacity to welcome and adopt talent from everywhere. I fear lately that we will recognize this strategic U.S. strength only once it is lost.
  The Times is committed to publishing   a diversity of letters  to the editor. We’d like to hear what you think about this or any of our articles. Here are some   tips . And here’s our email:   letters@nytimes.com .

Sumeru Equity Partners的负责人马克·哈勒(Mark Haller)告诉TechCrunch：“Sumeru Equity Partners正在对公司进行多数投资，但也为未来的增长充分利用了业务资本。”
根据Crunchbase的数据，该公司此前筹集了4700万美元。哈勒表示，这不是收购，而是与那些之前的投资者建立合作伙伴关系。他表示：“我们看到与现有投资者的持续合作，我们将进入并进行多数投资，我们还将在资产负债表上进行另一项投资。”
Sumeru首席执行官加里·中村(Gary Nakamura)表示，Sumeru得到的是一家利用营销技术帮助内部沟通的公司。Nakamura告诉TechCrunch：“作为一名领导者、管理者和组织，你可以通过目标细分和所有遥测来开展活动，以了解你的沟通是如何与员工沟通的。”
目标是大公司，客户包括福特、阿彻·丹尼尔斯·米德兰(Archer Daniels Midland)和波音(Boeing)等大牌公司。该公司报告说，它在全球拥有120个大客户，业务一直在以每年50%的速度增长。
中村表示，虽然公司从Sumeru那里获得了这笔现金注入，但他将继续努力，以周到的方式管理公司，这意味着在公司现有的120名员工之外，他们如何招聘员工要谨慎。
“我们所建立的是一项不需要很多人来经营的业务。通过我们已经实施的财务纪律，我们可以保持50%的增长率。从历史上看，这就是我们能够做到的。“他说。
Sumeru Equity Partners是一家总部位于旧金山的私募股权公司。根据公司网站的说法，它的目标是中端市场公司，然后试图通过在产品战略、加速上市和组织发展等领域与他们合作，应用运营效率，目标是建立公司并使其退出。
note_en:
“ Sumeru Equity Partners   is making a majority investment in the company but also well capitalizing the business for future growth,” Mark Haller, principal at Sumeru told TechCrunch.
 The company previously raised $47 million, according to Crunchbase data. Haller says this is not a buyout, so much as a partnership with those previous investors. “We’re seeing continued partnership with existing investors and we’re coming in and making that majority investment, and we’ll also be making another investment in the balance sheet,” he said.
  What Sumeru is getting is a company that helps with internal communications using marketing techniques, says company CEO Gary Nakamura. “You can run campaigns with targeting segmentation and all the telemetry back that you need as a leader, as a manager, as an organization to understand how your communications are landing with your workforce,” Nakamura told TechCrunch.
 The target is large companies and customers include big names like Ford, Archer Daniels Midland and Boeing. The company reports it has 120 large customers around the world, and the business has been growing at 50% year over year.
 While the company is getting this infusion of cash from Sumeru, Nakamura says he will continue to try and manage the company in a thoughtful way, and that means being careful about how they hire beyond the 120 employees the company already has.
 “What we have built is a business that doesn’t require a lot of heads to run it. We can maintain a 50% growth rate with financial discipline that we’ve implemented. Historically that is what we’ve been able to do,” he said.
 Sumeru Equity Partners is a private equity firm based in San Francisco. It targets mid-market companies, according to the company website and then tries to apply operational efficiency by working with them on areas like product strategy, go-to-market acceleration and organizational development with the goal of building up the company and taking it to exit.
title: Sumeru Equity Partners投资1亿美元收购SocialChorus多数股权
title_en: Sumeru Equity Partners buys majority stake in SocialChorus with $100M investment

该公司的租赁市场已经在洛杉矶拥有数万个单元，租户自己在其平台上对此进行了描述。今年晚些时候，该公司将扩展到旧金山和圣地亚哥，并计划在2021年搬到西雅图，同年晚些时候搬到纽约和芝加哥。
支持该公司扩张计划和发展的是一轮280万美元的种子资金，资金来自大卫·萨克斯的投资公司Craft Ventures，以及Abstract VC、Wonder Ventures和Zillow的联合创始人、最近从洛杉矶归来的斯宾塞·拉斯科夫(Spencer Rascoff)。其他未披露的天使投资者也参与了这轮融资。
PocketList仍在营收前，计划通过向物业经理和房东收取公寓潜在租户的线索来赚钱。房东可以向表示对房产感兴趣的潜在租房者发送出境信息，达维在谈到该公司计划提供的一项新服务时说。
PocketList投资人、dot.LA和Zillow创始人斯宾塞·拉斯科夫(Spencer Rascoff)表示：“给房地产带来透明度是一个近20年的过程，而公寓市场才刚刚起步。”“PocketList让透明度更上一层楼，将独家的‘售前’库存公之于众，租房者蜂拥使用这项服务也就不足为奇了。”
租房者可以免费下载PocketList应用程序，对他们目前的公寓进行评级，并访问该应用程序上提供的列表。与此同时，该公司表示，物业经理和房东可以使用专业版，它具有他们想要的所有外展、管理和概览功能。
“考虑到目前租赁市场的建立方式，租房者‘需要找地方’和实际‘租一个地方’之间几乎总是存在差距，”达泽继续说。“PocketList打破了这个循环，让人们比以往任何时候都更早地进入他们喜欢的家庭。”
note_en:
The company Dazé has built with co-founder  Julian Vergel de Dios, has, in fact, flipped the traditional script of relying on landlords and property managers to source information about available rentals and is relying on renters to provide information about the apartments they’re in… and the apartments where they’d like to be.
 PocketList started out as an experiment born out of a lot of trial and error, according to Dazé, but it was around the initial revelation that “renters typically know they’re going to be moving way ahead of time.”
  The service, which is available as an app and online, was a business nearly four years in the making. Initially Dazé and Vergel de Dios built a service called Block. “ The hypothesis with Block was that a big painpoint for renters was communication between people looking for apartments together,” Dazé said. “We made a tool that was well designed and well built … it was a cross between Slack and Microsoft Excel, but specifically for renters. The biggest problem was that it didn’t have a good business model.”
  What started as Block, eventually morphed into PocketList and the two Los Angeles-based founders quickly took the app to market with little fanfare in November. Growing initially by word of mouth, the service allows for an easy sign up where renters fill out a brief description of their apartment — basically letting the community know what it’s like and that it will soon be on the market — and then can search for other apartments in areas they’re interested in.
 Rentals typically sit vacant for an average of 26 days every time they turn over, according to PocketList’s internal data. That delay costs landlords as much as $43 billion and puts pressure on supply constrained housing markets, the company said. But using PocketList, renters can leave a place more quickly and owners reduce the time an apartments stands empty to perhaps one week.  Currently, the average unit on PocketList is rented 67 days before the landlord is given notice, and 97 days before the unit appears on other listing sites, the company said in a statement.
 For renters, the network provides information about apartments that will be available on the market and a way to query current occupants about potential units. Interested renters can also reach out directly to landlords and property managers via the PocketList app to indicate that they’d be interested in looking at a place. To ensure security no contact information is shared with a property manager until a renter submits an application and last names and images are hidden until the renter applies for an apartment.
 On the other side of the equation, landlords and property managers are able to claim properties for free and see feedback from renters about their buildings — and the thoughts renters have of other properties in their area. They also get earlier access to potential tenants once a previous tenant gives notice.
 The next features on the product roadmap include descriptions of areas which give potential renters the option to select for the kinds of neighborhood features they want and more tools for landlords like direct application submissions, renter resumes and other landlord response tools.
 The company’s rental marketplace already has tens of thousands of units in Los Angeles described on its platform by the renters themselves and will be expanding to San Francisco and San Diego later this year with plans to move into Seattle in 2021 and New York and Chicago later that same year.
 Backing the company’s expansion plans and development is a $2.8 million seed round which came from David Sacks’ investment firm, Craft ventures, along with Abstract VC, Wonder Ventures, and Zillow co-founder, and recent Los Angeles returnee, Spencer Rascoff. Other, undisclosed, angel investors also participated in the round.
 Still pre-revenue, PocketList plans to make money off of charging property managers and landlords for leads on likely renters for their apartments. Landlords can send outbound messages to potential renters that have expressed interest in a property, said Davé, of one of the company’s planned new services.
 “Bringing transparency to real estate has been a nearly 20-year process, and the apartment market is only just getting there,” said Spencer Rascoff, PocketList investor, and founder of dot.LA and Zillow. “PocketList takes transparency to the next level, bringing exclusive ‘pre-market’ inventory into the light, and it’s no surprise that renters have flocked to the service.”
 Renters can download the PocketList app for free, rate their current apartment and get access to the listings available on the app. Meanwhile, property managers and landlords have access to a pro version, which has all of the outreach, management and overview features that they’d want, the company said.
 “Given the way the current rental market is set up, there’s almost always a gap between when a renter ‘needs to find a place’ and actually ‘rents a place,” continued Dazé. “PocketList breaks the cycle and gets people into homes they love earlier than ever before.”
title: 洛杉矶的PocketList为租房者提供了更好、更快的信息，了解城市里有哪些公寓可用
title_en: LA’s PocketList gives renters better information, faster, about what apartments are available in cities

note:
根据Sensor Tower App Intelligence的数据，在对无许可证发布的游戏进行打击后，7月前7天，中国应用商店下架了2500多款手机游戏。
中国的手机游戏法规要求所有游戏在发布之前都必须获得国家新闻出版局的许可，这证实了这款应用符合国家对内容的严格规定。尽管如此，许多游戏之前都可以在没有获得批准的情况下在中国的App Store上发布。然而，现在苹果将遵守国家的规定，开发者必须在7月31日之前在App Store Connect的游戏页面上输入他们的批准号和支持文件。7月31日之后，在提供许可证号之前，高级游戏和任何包括没有ISBN的应用内购买的游戏都将从中国的应用商店中删除。
在本月的前7天，市场上增加了近660款游戏，同时有2500多款游戏被删除。相比之下，4月前7天被移除的游戏数量是退市数量的近4倍，而被移除的游戏数量是5月前7天的5倍，是6月同期的4倍多。请注意，我们的数据仅包含那些在某个时候足以在App Store的游戏子类别中排名，并且已被删除5天或更长时间的标题。在被删除的游戏中，自2012年1月1日以来，近2000款游戏(80%)在中国的下载量不到1万次。
7月第一周下架的这些游戏在中国总共产生了3470万美元的终身毛收入，其中一款累积超过1000万美元，只有六款收入超过100万美元。这两款游戏在中国总共获得了1.334亿次终身下载量，其中一款游戏的安装量超过1000万次，而30款游戏的总下载量超过100万次。
在此期间，中国应用商店下架的著名游戏包括Glu的Contract Killer Zombies 2，Zynga的Solitaire，Crazy Labs的ASMR Slting，以及Flaregames的不间断Chuck Norris。在这个数据集之外的最近几天，Supercell的Hay Day也被从市场上撤下。
多年来，中国一直是世界上最有利可图的手机游戏市场，在App Store上创造了这一类别收入最高的国家。2019年，中国应用商店上的游戏估计产生了126亿美元的收入，占去年苹果市场全球游戏支出的33.2%。排名第二的国家是美国，积累了90亿美元，占App Store游戏总收入的23.7%。与此同时，在2020年上半年，中国的App Store积累了67亿美元，占全球总额的30%，而美国商店积累了58亿美元，占26%。
就整个市场而言，从被移除的游戏中产生的3470万美元的终生收入并不都是那么重要。然而，随着发行商获得ISBN的最后期限的临近，我们可以预期在下个月会看到更多的下架，对国际公司来说，在中国发布他们的游戏将比以往任何时候都更具挑战性。

note_en:
More than 2,500 mobile games were removed from China’s App Store in the first seven days of July following a crackdown on titles that are available without a license for release, according to Sensor Tower  App Intelligence data.
  China’s mobile gaming regulations require that all titles receive a license from the country’s National Press and Publication Administration before release, confirming that the app meets the country’s strict rules on content. Despite this, many titles were previously able to release on China’s App Store without such approval. Now, however, Apple will be adhering to the nation’s regulations, and developers will have until July 31 to enter their approval number and supporting documentation to their game’s page in App Store Connect. After July 31, premium games and any title that includes in-app purchases without an ISBN will be removed from China’s App Store until a license number is provided.
 In the first seven days of this month, close to 660 games have been added to the marketplace, while more than 2,500 titles have been removed. For comparison, close to four times as many games have been removed than were delisted in the first seven days of April, while there have been five times more removals than in the first seven days of May, and over four times more than the same period in June. Note that our figures only capture titles that were large enough to rank in the App Store’s games subcategories at some point and that have been removed for five days or more. Of the games removed, nearly 2,000 titles, or 80 percent, had generated less than 10,000 downloads in China since January 1, 2012.
  The games removed in the first week of July had generated a combined $34.7 million in lifetime gross revenue in China, with one accumulating more than $10 million, and just six that earned over $1 million. The titles had racked up a combined 133.4 million lifetime downloads in China, with one title generating more than 10 million installs, while 30 had picked up over one million.
 Notable games removed from China’s App Store during this period include  Contract Killer Zombies 2 from  Glu,  Solitaire from  Zynga,  ASMR Slicing from  Crazy Labs, and  Nonstop Chuck Norris from  Flaregames. In more recent days outside this dataset,  Hay Day from  Supercell was also taken down from the marketplace.
  China has for years been the most lucrative mobile games market in the world, and generated the highest revenue of any country on the App Store for the category. In 2019, games on China’s App Store generated an estimated $12.6 billion, representing 33.2 percent of all global games spending on Apple’s marketplace last year. The No. 2 country was the United States, which accumulated $9 billion, or 23.7 percent of total App Store games revenue. In the first half of 2020, meanwhile, China’s App Store has racked up $6.7 billion, or 30 percent of the worldwide total, while the U.S. store has accumulated $5.8 billion, or 26 percent.
 In terms of the overall market, the $34.7 million in lifetime revenue generated from the removed games is not all that significant. As the deadline looms for publishers to obtain an ISBN, however, we can expect to see further removals during the next month and it will be more challenging than ever for international companies to release their games in China.
 Sensor Tower’s Store Intelligence platform is an Enterprise level offering. Interested in learning more?
title: 分析：苹果中国App Store 7月前七日下架2500多款手游，此前打击无授权游戏
title_en: Analysis: 2,500+ mobile games were removed from Apple's App Store in China in the first seven days of July, following a crackdown on unlicensed games

note:
华盛顿(路透社)-奥利弗·泰勒(Oliver Taylor)是英国伯明翰大学(University Of Birmingham)的一名学生，20多岁，棕色眼睛，浅胡茬，微笑略显僵硬。
网上个人资料将他描述为在一个传统的犹太家庭长大的咖啡爱好者和政治瘾君子。他的六篇自由社论和博客帖子表明，他对反犹太主义和犹太事务有着积极的兴趣，“耶路撒冷邮报”和“以色列时报”都有署名。
他的大学说没有他的记录。除了在问答网站Quora上的账户外，他没有明显的在线足迹，3月份他在Quora活跃了两天。两家发表了他的作品的报纸说，他们试图确认他的身份，但失败了。欺骗性图像方面的专家使用最先进的法医分析程序确定泰勒的头像是超逼真的赝品--“深度赝品”。
路透社还不知道谁是泰勒的幕后黑手。拨打他提供给编辑的英国电话号码，收到了一条自动错误信息，他没有回复他用来通信的Gmail地址留下的信息。
伦敦学者马赞·马斯里(Mazen Masri)向路透社(Reuters)通报了泰勒的消息。2018年末，马斯里代表监控公司NSO所谓的墨西哥受害者帮助以色列对该公司电话窃听技术的受害者提起诉讼，引起了国际社会的关注。
在美国犹太报纸The Algemeiner的一篇文章中，泰勒指责马斯里和他的妻子，巴勒斯坦权利活动家里夫卡·巴纳德(Ryvka Barnard)是“众所周知的恐怖分子同情者”。
马斯里和巴纳德对这一指控大吃一惊，他们否认了这一指控。但他们也不明白为什么一个大学生会把他们挑出来。马斯里说他调出了泰勒的头像。他说，他不能用手指指着它，但这个年轻人的脸“似乎有些不对劲”。
在加州大学伯克利分校任教的数字图像取证先驱哈尼·法里德(Hany Farid)表示：“背景中的扭曲和不一致是合成图像的一个明显迹象，他的脖子和衣领上的一些小故障也是如此。”哈尼·法里德(Hany Farid)在加州大学伯克利分校(University of California，Berkeley)任教。
经常在他的作品中使用深伪的艺术家马里奥·克林格曼(Mario Klingemann)表示，这张照片“具有所有的特征”。
这些面孔不是真的许多“深假”看起来很奇怪，但技术正在快速发展。
泰勒的角色是一种现象的罕见例子，这种现象已经成为数字时代的一种关键焦虑：深度假货和虚假信息的结合。
这一威胁在华盛顿和硅谷引起了越来越多的关注。去年，众议院情报委员会(House Intelligence Committee)主席亚当·希夫(Adam Schiff)警告说，计算机生成的视频可能会“把世界领导人变成口技表演者的假人”。上个月，Facebook宣布了其深伪检测挑战赛的结束-这是一项旨在帮助研究人员自动识别伪造镜头的比赛。上周，在线出版物“每日野兽”(The Daily Beast)揭露了一个深度虚假记者网络--这是一个更大的虚假人物角色在网上散布宣传种子的集团的一部分。
丹·布拉米(Dan Brahmy)说，像泰勒这样的深假照片是危险的，因为它们可以帮助建立“一个完全无法追踪的身份”。他的以色列初创公司Cyabra专门检测这类图像。
布拉米说，追查这类照片来源的调查人员剩下的工作是“大海捞针--除非针不存在。”
泰勒似乎在12月下旬开始写文章之前一直没有出现在网上。伯明翰大学在一份声明中说，他们找不到“这个人使用这些细节的任何记录”。耶路撒冷邮报和阿尔盖迈纳的编辑说，泰勒通过电子邮件向他们抛出冷冰冰的故事后，他们发表了泰勒的文章。他们说，他没有要求付款，他们也没有采取激进的措施来审查他的身份。
“我们不是反间谍行动，”Algemeiner主编Dovid Efune说，尽管他指出该报自那以后引入了新的保障措施。
在路透社开始询问泰勒的情况后，阿尔盖迈纳和以色列时报删除了他的作品。泰勒给两家报纸发了电子邮件，抗议撤职，但以色列时报的观点编辑米里亚姆·赫施拉格(Miriam Herschlag)表示，在他未能证明自己的身份后，她断然拒绝了他。Efune说，他没有回复泰勒的信息。
“耶路撒冷邮报”和阿鲁茨·舍瓦将泰勒的文章保留在网上，尽管后者在马斯里和巴纳德投诉后删除了“恐怖分子同情者”的提法。当被问及泰勒的作品是否会持续下去时，邮报主编雅科夫·卡茨(Yaakov Katz)没有回答。Arutz Sheva编辑约尼·凯宾斯基(Yoni Kempinski)只表示，“在许多情况下”，新闻机构“使用化名来署名发表评论文章”。凯宾斯基拒绝详细说明，也拒绝透露他是否认为泰勒是化名。
奥利弗·泰勒的文章在社交媒体上的参与度很低，但以色列“赫施拉格时报”表示，这些文章仍然是危险的-不仅因为它们可能扭曲公共话语，还因为它们可能会让处于她这个位置的人不太愿意在不知名的作家身上冒险。
“我们绝对需要过滤掉冒名顶替者，加强我们的防御，”她说。“但我不想设置这些障碍，阻碍新的声音被听到。”
note_en:
WASHINGTON (Reuters) - Oliver Taylor, a student at England’s University of Birmingham, is a twenty-something with brown eyes, light stubble, and a slightly stiff smile.
  Online profiles describe him as a coffee lover and politics junkie who was raised in a traditional Jewish home. His half dozen freelance editorials and blog posts reveal an active interest in anti-Semitism and Jewish affairs, with bylines in the Jerusalem Post and the Times of Israel.
  His university says it has no record of him. He has no obvious online footprint beyond an account on the question-and-answer site Quora, where he was active for two days in March. Two newspapers that published his work say they have tried and failed to confirm his identity. And experts in deceptive imagery used state-of-the-art forensic analysis programs to determine that Taylor’s profile photo is a hyper-realistic forgery - a “deepfake.”
 Who is behind Taylor isn’t known to Reuters. Calls to the U.K. phone number he supplied to editors drew an automated error message and he didn’t respond to messages left at the Gmail address he used for correspondence.
 Reuters was alerted to Taylor by London academic Mazen Masri, who drew international attention in late 2018 when he helped launch an Israeli lawsuit against the surveillance company NSO on behalf of alleged Mexican victims of the company’s phone hacking technology.
 In an article in U.S. Jewish newspaper The Algemeiner, Taylor had accused Masri and his wife, Palestinian rights campaigner Ryvka Barnard, of being “known terrorist sympathizers.”
 Masri and Barnard were taken aback by the allegation, which they deny. But they were also baffled as to why a university student would single them out. Masri said he pulled up Taylor’s profile photo. He couldn’t put his finger on it, he said, but something about the young man’s face “seemed off.”
  “The distortion and inconsistencies in the background are a tell-tale sign of a synthesized image, as are a few glitches around his neck and collar,” said digital image forensics pioneer Hany Farid, who teaches at the University of California, Berkeley.
 Artist Mario Klingemann, who regularly uses deepfakes in his work, said the photo “has all the hallmarks.”
   These faces are not real Many “deepfakes” look odd, but technology is fast improving
  The Taylor persona is a rare in-the-wild example of a phenomenon that has emerged as a key anxiety of the digital age: The marriage of deepfakes and disinformation.
 The threat is drawing increasing concern in Washington and Silicon Valley. Last year House Intelligence Committee chairman Adam Schiff warned that computer-generated video could “turn a world leader into a ventriloquist’s dummy.” Last month Facebook announced the conclusion of its Deepfake Detection Challenge - a competition intended to help researchers automatically identify falsified footage. Last week online publication The Daily Beast revealed a network of deepfake journalists - part of a larger group of bogus personas seeding propaganda online.
 Deepfakes like Taylor are dangerous because they can help build “a totally untraceable identity,” said Dan Brahmy, whose Israel-based startup Cyabra specializes in detecting such images.
 Brahmy said investigators chasing the origin of such photos are left “searching for a needle in a haystack – except the needle doesn’t exist.”
 Taylor appears to have had no online presence until he started writing articles in late December. The University of Birmingham said in a statement it could not find “any record of this individual using these details.” Editors at the Jerusalem Post and The Algemeiner say they published Taylor after he pitched them stories cold over email. He didn’t ask for payment, they said, and they didn’t take aggressive steps to vet his identity.
 “We’re not a counterintelligence operation,” Algemeiner Editor-in-chief Dovid Efune said, although he noted that the paper had introduced new safeguards since.
 After Reuters began asking about Taylor, The Algemeiner and the Times of Israel deleted his work. Taylor emailed both papers protesting the removal, but Times of Israel Opinion Editor Miriam Herschlag said she rebuffed him after he failed to prove his identity. Efune said he didn’t respond to Taylor’s messages.
  The Jerusalem Post and Arutz Sheva have kept Taylor’s articles online, although the latter removed the “terrorist sympathizers” reference following a complaint from Masri and Barnard. The Post’s editor-in-chief, Yaakov Katz, didn’t respond when asked whether Taylor’s work would stay up. Arutz Sheva editor Yoni Kempinski said only that “in many cases” news outlets “use pseudonyms to byline opinion articles.” Kempinski declined to elaborate or say whether he considered Taylor a pseudonym.
 Oliver Taylor’s articles drew minimal engagement on social media, but the Times of Israel’s Herschlag said they were still dangerous - not only because they could distort the public discourse but also because they risked making people in her position less willing to take chances on unknown writers.
 “Absolutely we need to screen out impostors and up our defenses,” she said. “But I don’t want to set up these barriers that prevent new voices from being heard.”
title: 用于攻击活动人士夫妇的Deepfalse显示了新的虚假信息前沿
title_en: Deepfake used to attack activist couple shows new disinformation frontier

Charge Communications已向美国联邦通信委员会(FCC)请愿，请求允许在FCC的上限禁令到期至少两年前对客户实施数据上限。FCC禁止上限是这家有线电视公司为换取2016年批准其与时代华纳有线电视(Time Warner Cable)和光明屋网络(Bright House Networks)的合并而施加的一项关键条件。
2016年，FCC告诉Spectrum，如果不要求一些有利于您作为Spectrum客户的改变和条件，它的合并不符合公众利益。因为FCC认识到有线电视行业的竞争并不常见，它知道合并后会有一种诱惑，那就是对互联网客户设置数据上限，除了公司可以这样做之外，没有任何好的理由。事实上，数据上限长期以来一直被讨论为一种威慑，以防止客户放弃有线电视订阅，转而使用流媒体视频。为什么？因为如果你流式播放Netflix、Hulu、YouTube TV、Sling和其他公司的电视节目，这些数据使用量将很快耗尽Spectrum在其数据上限中包括的任何数据限额。大多数有数据上限的公司，如果你超过你的津贴，都会确保你支付高昂的费用。事实上的标准超限费是每50 GB 10美元，最高可达每月100-200美元！这种账单冲击很可能会把你推回有线电视。
FCC希望，对Spectrum实施数据上限的七年禁令将给竞争一个发展的机会，而不仅仅是在流媒体视频方面。事实上，FCC辩称，新进入的有线电视运营商、家庭提供商的光纤和5G服务可能会造成如此激烈的竞争，数据上限可能会消失。不幸的是，在过去的四年里，消费者几乎没有看到竞争的出现。事实上，许多人仍然只有一个选择-有线电视垄断-互联网服务达到FCC的最低速度(25 Mbps)才有资格成为宽带。电话公司的DSL很少能提供当地有线电视运营商提供的速度。在一些地区，光纤到户的竞争正在加剧，但许多家庭仍然缺乏接入。尽管媒体对5G大肆炒作，但在未来几年内，强大而快速的无线家庭互联网只会在一小部分家庭中使用。
尽管存在这一现实，查特还是要求联邦通信委员会让数据上限禁令提前两年到期，这意味着他们可以在明年春天之前对像你这样的客户设置数据上限。查特认为，现在有很多流媒体服务在争夺你的业务，所以没有证据表明Spectrum正在损害流媒体电视的市场。因此，没有必要保护消费者不受数据上限的影响。
大多数大型流媒体视频提供商的所有者是大型卫星、有线电视和电话公司(康卡斯特的孔雀、AT&amp；T的TV/TV Now和HBO Max、Dish Network的Sling TV)、大型电视集团(ABC-迪士尼的Hulu/Disney+、哥伦比亚广播公司-维亚康姆的All Access)或科技公司(Apple TV、YouTube TV)。Netflix提高了服务价格，部分原因是它被迫向康卡斯特等有线电视公司支付“互连费”，以保证康卡斯特的客户获得合适的服务。大多数与电信公司没有关联的流媒体服务一直反对数据上限，因为他们知道数据上限可能会反竞争，损害订户数量。
什么比赛？Charge Spectrum客户可能仍然拥有与2016年相同的竞争选择(如果有的话)，这是不够的。对家庭宽带服务设置数据上限表明，行动中缺乏竞争。康卡斯特避免对竞争更激烈的东北部和大西洋中部地区的客户设置数据上限，该公司在这些地区面对的是Verizon的FiOS服务，该服务没有数据上限。
查特要求并批准了消费者不需要或不想要的合并。查特自愿同意了FCC的条件，以完成交易。协议就是协议，但查特现在想要离开。该公司正花费数千美元聘请律师，以摆脱FCC的数据上限禁令，同时声称他们没有实施数据上限的计划。你真的相信他们吗？
消费者讨厌数据上限。事实上，仅仅是对互联网服务设置数据上限就会破坏提供商的营销和广告活动，并使签约新客户变得困难。有数据上限的公司比没有数据上限的公司流失更多的客户，因为如果有新的无上限竞争对手到来，客户就会更换。仅仅是处理安装复杂的使用表和让抱怨其准确性的客户感到不安的成本，就比公司从超额费中赚取的任何收入都要高。值得注意的是，这些不仅仅是“阻止封顶”的观点！查特自己告诉FCC，这些只是一些反对实施数据上限的强有力的商业理由。现在，它正在请求联邦通信委员会允许实施数据上限，尽管如此！
数据上限并不能保护宽带网络不受拥塞的影响，它们也不是关于公平共享互联网容量的问题。正在进行的大流行刚刚证明，大型有线电视和电话公司现有的宽带网络完全有能力处理网络流量的大幅增长。合理、经济高效的升级将在未来几年继续这一成功故事，而不需要任意的数据上限。别搞错了。数据上限只是电信公司将你的使用货币化以增加他们已经丰厚的利润的另一种方式。
在2020年7月22日之前，您可以直接向FCC发送评论，敦促他们不要允许查特要求提前结束合并交易条件。门罗县(纽约州)。立法委员雷切尔·巴恩哈特和阻止上尉！已经联手将这条信息传递给各地的Spectrum客户。我们需要提醒FCC，它必须保持足够好的独立性，并允许交易条件保持不变。我们还希望向Charge的高管发出一个明确的信息，即客户不想要数据上限…。永远不会。这是一条信息让帽子停下来！2009年成功交付给时代华纳有线的最高领导层，他们倾听了。现在是时候向查特的人们发出另一个信息了。我们真诚地希望他们也会倾听。
这里有一封信的样本，我们敦促您在提交之前调整一下，以反映您自己的观点和情况：
请拒绝查特的要求，将其同意的交易条件作为其与时代华纳有线电视和光明之家网络合并的一部分。
交易就是交易，查特同意至少在七年内不对客户设定数据上限。它现在希望提前两年解除禁令，辩称竞争在过去四年里蓬勃发展。事实上，对我们来说，几乎没有什么变化。竞争并没有蓬勃发展。我们仍然没有宽带服务的选择，尽管有更多的流媒体视频提供商，但大多数都是由大型有线电视、卫星和电话公司或大型媒体集团拥有的。数据上限会让我重新考虑使用这些服务，因为我负担不起更高的互联网账单。
在一个健康的市场中，竞争应该会降低价格。但是我的账单只会上涨。在告诉所有人他们的网络足够强大，可以应对越来越多的在家使用之后，什么样的公司会要求允许在大流行期间对客户施加使用限制？答案是，这家公司面临的竞争很少，而且不担心竞争对手会利用这一要求来对付他们。互联网的可负担性已经是一个巨大的问题，而数据上限只会让互联网服务变得更加昂贵。我们已经为服务支付了世界上最高的价格之一。
我的家人没有要求这次合并，FCC在2016年认定，在没有施加几个条件让消费者从交易中受益的情况下批准合并不符合公众利益。FCC应该坚持宪章是信守诺言的，而不是设置数据上限。查特在2016年告诉FCC，它“厌恶数据上限”，称它没有实施使用限制，而是选择将没有数据上限作为一种竞争优势进行营销“，并表示”不实施上限有很强的商业理由“，而且上限”破坏了“其营销信息。查特在2016年对FCC诚实吗？他们目前要求允许取消数据上限的请求似乎忽略了查特本身几年前对FCC采取的立场。
我们敦促您拒绝查特的请愿书，该请愿书将允许查特继续从无限制互联网接入的销售中赚取大量资金，并继续履行其“没有数据上限”的互联网服务广告承诺，就像它现在所做的那样。

note_en:
Charter Communications has petitioned the FCC for permission to impose DATA CAPS on customers at least two years before the FCC’s prohibition on caps — a key condition imposed on the cable company in return for approval of its 2016 merger with Time Warner Cable and Bright House Networks — is scheduled to expire.
 In 2016, the FCC told Spectrum its merger was NOT in the public interest without requiring some changes and conditions that would benefit you as a Spectrum customer. Because the FCC recognized that competition was uncommon in the cable industry, it knew there would be a temptation after a merger to slap data caps on internet customers for no good reason, other than the fact the company could. In fact, data caps have long been discussed as a deterrent to keep customers from dropping cable TV subscriptions in favor of streaming video. Why? Because if you stream TV programming from Netflix, Hulu, YouTube TV, Sling, and others, that data usage would quickly eat up any data allowances Spectrum would include with its data cap. Most companies with data caps make sure you pay dearly if you go over your allowance. The de facto standard overlimit fee is $10 for each 50 GB of usage, up to a maximum ranging between $100-200 a month! That kind of bill shock would likely push you back to cable TV.
 The FCC hoped that a seven-year ban on Spectrum imposing data caps would give competition a chance to develop, and not just with streaming video. In fact, the FCC argued newly arriving cable operators, fiber to the home providers, and 5G services could probably create so much competition, data caps would likely disappear. Unfortunately, consumers have seen little competition emerge in the last four years. In fact, many still have only one choice — a cable monopoly — for internet service that meets the FCC’s minimum speed (25 Mbps) to qualify as broadband. DSL from the phone company rarely provides the speed available from your local cable operator. Fiber to the home competition is growing in some areas, but many homes still lack access. Although there has been much hype in the media about 5G, robust and fast wireless home internet will only be available in a fraction of homes for years to come.
 Despite this reality, Charter is asking the FCC to let the ban on data caps expire two years early, which means they could slap data caps on customers just like you by next spring. Charter argues there are lots of streaming services now competing for your business, so there is no evidence Spectrum is hurting the marketplace for streaming television. Therefore, there is no need to protect consumers from data caps.
   Most large streaming video providers are owned by giant satellite, cable and telephone companies (Comcast’s Peacock, AT&T’s TV/TV Now and HBO Max, Dish Network’s Sling TV), giant TV conglomerates (ABC-Disney’s Hulu/Disney +, CBS-Viacom’s All Access), or tech companies (Apple TV, YouTube TV). Netflix has raised prices for its service, in part because it has been pushed to pay cable companies like Comcast “interconnection fees” to guarantee Comcast customers will get suitable service. Most streaming services not affiliated with telecom companies have opposed data caps all along, understanding they can be anticompetitive and hurt subscriber numbers.
 What competition? Charter Spectrum customers likely still have the same competitive options they had in 2016, if any, which is not enough. Imposing data caps on home broadband service illustrates that lack of competition in action. Comcast has avoided imposing data caps on its customers in the more competitive northeast and mid-Atlantic regions, where it faces Verizon’s FiOS service, which does not have data caps.
 Charter asked for and was granted approval of a merger consumers did not need or want. Charter voluntarily agreed to the FCC’s conditions to close the deal. A deal is a deal, but Charter now wants to walk away. The company is spending thousands on its attorneys to free itself from the FCC’s data cap ban while claiming they have no plans to implement data caps. Do you honestly believe them?
 Consumers hate data caps. In fact, just having data caps on internet service can undermine a provider’s marketing and ad campaigns and make signing up new customers difficult. Companies with data caps lose more customers than those that don’t because customers switch if a new cap-free competitor comes to town. Just dealing with implementing complicated usage meters and upset customers complaining about their accuracy costs more than any revenue companies earn from overlimit fees. Remarkably, those are not just the views of  Stop the Cap! Charter  itself told the FCC those were just some reasons there was a strong business case against implementing data caps. Now it is asking the FCC for permission to impose data caps despite all that!
  Data caps do not protect broadband networks from congestion, and they are not about equitably sharing internet capacity. The ongoing pandemic just proved that big cable and phone companies have existing broadband networks more than capable of handling a large spike in network traffic. Reasonable, cost-effective upgrades will continue that success story for years to come with no need for arbitrary data caps. Make no mistake. Data caps are just another way telecom companies can monetize your usage to increase their already fat profits.
  Until July 22, 2020, you can send a comment directly to the FCC urging them NOT to allow Charter’s request to sunset merger deal conditions early. Monroe County (N.Y.) legislator Rachel Barnhart and  Stop the Cap! have teamed up to push this message through to Spectrum customers everywhere. We need to put the FCC on notice it must leave well enough alone and allow the deal conditions to remain in place. We also want to send a clear message to executives at Charter that customers  do not want data caps… ever. It’s a message  Stop the Cap! successfully delivered in 2009 to the top leadership of Time Warner Cable, and they listened. It’s now time to send another message to the folks at Charter. We sincerely hope they will listen too.
 Here is a sample letter, which we urge you to adjust to reflect your own views and circumstances before submitting:
  Please reject Charter’s request to sunset the deal conditions it agreed to as part of its merger with Time Warner Cable and Bright House Networks.
 A deal is a deal, and Charter agreed not to impose data caps on its customers for at least seven years. It now wants that prohibition lifted two years early, arguing competition has flourished over the last four years. In fact, little has changed for us. Competition has not flourished. We still do not have choices for broadband service and although there are more streaming video providers, most are owned by large cable, satellite, and phone companies or giant media conglomerates. Data caps will make me reconsider using these services because I cannot afford an even higher internet bill.
 Competition is supposed to bring pricing down in a healthy marketplace. But my bill is only going up. What kind of company would ask for permission to slap usage limits on customers in the middle of a pandemic, after telling everyone their networks were more than robust enough to handle increased stay-at-home usage? The answer is a company that faces little competition and has no fear a competitor will use this request against them. Internet affordability is already an enormous problem, and data caps just make internet service even more expensive. We already pay among the highest prices in the world for service.
 My family did not ask for this merger, and the FCC in 2016 determined it was not in the public interest to approve it without imposing a handful of conditions to allow consumers to benefit from the transaction. The FCC should insist Charter be true to its word and not impose data caps. Charter told the FCC in 2016 it had an “aversion to data caps, stating that instead of enforcing usage limits it chooses to market the absence of data caps as a competitive advantage” and that “there is a strong business case for not implementing caps” and that caps “undermined” its marketing messaging. Was Charter being honest with the FCC in 2016? Their current request for permission to lift data caps seems to ignore the positions Charter itself took with the FCC just a few years ago.
 We urge you to deny Charter’s petition, which will allow Charter to continue making plenty of money from the sale of unlimited internet access and continue honoring its advertising commitments to sell internet service “with no data caps” as it does now.

note:
计算机越来越善于想象“深度假象”--使用一种叫做“生成性对抗网络”(GAN)的技术创造出的照片真实感人脸。
情报顾问穆尼拉·穆斯塔法(Munira MuStaa)表示，这项技术使宣传员能够“在没有任何历史或包袱”的情况下，隐藏在计算机生成的人物角色后面。穆尼拉·穆斯塔法之前曾在野外寻找过这样的图像。
7月15日，路透社(Reuters)报道了一位据称名叫奥利弗·泰勒(Oliver Taylor)的英国作家，专家表示，他具备由GAN制作的所有特征。记者试图联系泰勒或他背后的任何人，但均未成功。
这里有一本图文并茂的指南，介绍什么是深伪，以及如何识别它们。
构建人工智能化身的初创公司Pincreen的首席执行官兼联合创始人李浩表示，生成性对抗性网络是指经过反复试错的决斗计算机程序。
一个名为生成器的程序按顺序向一张脸发出数百万次尝试；第二个程序名为鉴别器，试图嗅出第一个程序的脸是否是假的。李说，如果鉴别者无法辨别，就会产生深度假。
据路透社就深度假图像接受路透社采访的六位专家表示，甘氏总体上擅长模仿人，但一些面部特征仍然给他们带来了麻烦，导致许多人工生成的肖像在牙齿、眼睛和耳朵周围出现了签名毛刺。告密性的怪胎可能会潜入衣服、配饰或背景中，而甘斯也在为这些东西而苦苦挣扎。以下是专家所说的是观众正在看GaN图像的线索。
模糊、超现实的背景是一个直接的危险信号。在泰勒的例子中，他身后墙上的瓷砖或接缝似乎在起伏和消散。
甘斯很难给他们的作品穿上衣服。眼镜的镜框不对称，耳环很少搭配。在泰勒的肖像画中，他的领子有华夫饼图案(左边)和光滑的面料(右边)。
甘斯创造的嘴巴通常看起来很畸形，而且可能有多余的牙齿。泰勒右侧的下巴似乎有一颗模糊的牙齿，他的一颗左前磨牙似乎与他的下嘴唇融合在一起。
当GAN的头发不好的时候，它就会显示出来。毛发可以从额头上长出，也可以从耳朵和脖子上长出。泰勒的长长的飞行路线是GaN图像有时炫耀的不同寻常的头发的典型特征。泰勒还有其他问题。他的皮肤在鼻子下面和下巴上有棕色的数字文物。照亮他脸部的强光似乎同时从两个不同的角度照射到他的眼睛。另一个能说明问题的GAN故障-一个水滴状的斑点-似乎卡在了他的左前磨牙牙齿之间。
李预测，深度假的创作者很快就能解决他和其他人在泰勒头像中发现的数字错误。“能力和质量将会提高，”他说。“一年后，如果有什么东西能解决所有这些奇怪的问题，我不会感到惊讶。”
注：开场视频中泰勒之前的图像是由汤森路透实验室数据科学家利兹·罗曼生成的
note_en:
Computers are getting better at dreaming up “deepfakes” — photorealistic human faces created using a technology called a generative adversarial network, or GAN.
 The technology is giving propagandists the ability to hide behind computer-generated personas “without any history or baggage,” said intelligence consultant Munira Mustaffa, who has previously hunted out such images in the wild.
 On July 15, Reuters reported on a purported British writer by the name of Oliver Taylor, who experts say bore all the hallmarks of having been produced by a GAN. Attempts to reach Taylor, or whoever was behind him, were unsuccessful.
 Here’s an illustrated guide on what deepfakes are — and how to spot them.
  A generative adversarial network is the name given to dueling computer programs that run through a process of trial and error, according to Hao Li, chief executive and cofounder of Pinscreen, a startup that builds AI avatars.
 One program, the generator, sequentially fires out millions of attempts at a face; the second program, the discriminator, tries to sniff out whether the first program’s face is a fake. If the discriminator can’t tell, Li said, a deepfake is produced.
  GANs are generally good at imitating people, but some facial features still give them trouble, leaving many artificially generated portraits with signature glitches around the teeth, eyes, and ears, according to six experts interviewed by Reuters about deepfake imagery. Telltale oddities can creep in to the clothes, accessories, or background, which GANs also struggle with. Here’s what the experts say are clues that a viewer is looking at a GAN image.
  Fuzzy, surreal backdrops are an immediate red flag. In Taylor’s case, the tiles or seams in the wall behind him seem to undulate and dissipate.
  GANs have trouble clothing their creations. Glasses have asymmetric frames; earrings rarely match. In Taylor’s portrait, his collar has a waffle pattern (on the left) and smooth fabric (on the right.)
  Mouths created by GANs often look misshapen, and can have excess teeth. The right side of Taylor’s jaw appeared to have a blurry tooth and one of his left premolars seemed to merge with his lower lip.
  When a GAN has a bad hair day, it shows. Hairs can sprout from foreheads or spike out from ears and necks. Taylor’s long flyaways are typical of the unusual hair sometimes sported by GAN images. Taylor has other problems too. His skin sports brown-colored digital artifacts under his nose and on his chin. And the bright light illuminating his face appears to be hitting his eyes from two different angles at once. Another telltale GAN glitch — a water droplet-like blob — appears to be stuck between his left premolar teeth.
  Li predicted that deepfake creators will soon be able to resolve the digital errors he and others spotted in Taylor’s profile photo. “Capabilities and quality will improve,” he said. “I wouldn’t be surprised in a year to have something that fixes all these weird issues.”
 NOTE: The images preceding Taylor in the opening video were generated by Thomson Reuters Labs data scientist Liz Roman
title: 路透社发布识别深度假个人资料照片指南
title_en: Reuters releases guide to recognizing deepfake profile photos

每一家新的流媒体服务都在寻找突出的功能，以使自己从竞争对手中脱颖而出，而孔雀的功能试图将三种不同类型的体验整合到一个应用程序中：传统的流媒体界面，小而短的新闻时刻的策划，以及“电视直播”。但在使用孔雀应用程序几天后，对我来说非常明显的是，这款应用程序并没有康卡斯特对它的雄心壮志那么强大。
根据人们观看孔雀的方式，他们将看到两个不同的观看选项。对于在电视上使用孔雀的人来说，这款应用程序会自动在主要的流媒体门户“浏览”中打开。在下面的左图中可以看到iPhone XR上正在使用的Browse版本。对于使用移动设备或平板电脑的人来说，孔雀将在“趋势”(Trending)中开放，它收集了简短的新闻、娱乐和体育剪辑。可以把它想象成Instagram、YouTube和Quibi的混合体。(Verge得到的是该应用程序的预览版，因此一些内容不可用。)。
(披露：拥有NBC环球的康卡斯特也是The Verge的母公司Vox Media的投资者。)。
让我们从Browse开始，因为许多人可能会通过他们的电视机、PlayStation和Xbox游戏机等联网设备，或者Apple TV设备来流媒体孔雀。Browse部分不是特别有创意，也不是特别令人印象深刻，但它是有效的。任何使用过Netflix或Hulu的人都已经对如何使用这款应用的这一特定部分有了具体的了解。然而，它真正实现的是展示NBC环球令人印象深刻的目录。这是一个IP宝库，以一种易于导航的方式布局，将大量优秀的电视节目聚集在一个地方。
孔雀的主要重点是NBC环球的图书目录。考虑到孔雀推出时没有太多的原著，拥有一个心爱的和需求旺盛的书库是必不可少的。每一排都是为了提醒订户，NBC环球什么都有，从真人秀节目(包括真人秀节目“真正的家庭主妇”和“与卡戴珊一家同行”)，到广受好评的喜剧节目(“30摇滚”、“两个半男人”、“人人都爱雷蒙德”)，再到程序类节目(法律与秩序：SVU、法律与秩序：犯罪意图)以及竞赛和房屋翻新节目。
通过这种方式，孔雀成功地提醒了人们，为什么它应该成为一款他们会持续打开的应用程序。前四个选项卡-特色、电视节目、电影和儿童-迎合了这些观众。浏览部分的第五个和第六个选项卡提供了更多关于孔雀如何将自己与其竞争对手区分开来的更多洞察力，而不仅仅是内容。
孔雀有一个专门的新闻和体育标签，大多数时候，这些标签上都会有直播节目。在新闻方面，顶部的磁贴-孔雀的团队称页面顶部的主要时段-很可能会改为NBC新宣布的24小时今日秀节目，今日全天。对于体育，将使用类似的设计，但当涉及到像英超比赛这样备受瞩目的独家赛事时，这些现场直播活动将在Browse主页的顶部有自己的瓷砖，上面突出地宣传着比赛。
如果孔雀的内容保险库是它最大的吸引力，为什么手机版专注于它的短片“趋势”页面？我不能告诉你。这个部分没有什么实质性的东西。它不能解决NBC环球的Twitter、Instagram和YouTube页面尚未解决的任何问题。
我能看到突出显示该部分的唯一原因是因为它感觉像是应该在电话上显示的东西。这些剪辑很短，向下滚动它们已经很容易了。NBC环球还可以使用这一部分向孔雀订户指出正在发生的事情；例如，英超现场比赛将在Trending选项卡中如此标记，供希望投身游戏的人使用。
根据我使用这款应用的时间，我想人们不会经常使用Trending。也就是说，孔雀团队正在研究扩展其功能和产品的方法，该团队将其视为帮助将这项服务与竞争对手区分开来的一种方式。“趋势”栏目还有助于打入孔雀真正想要向订户推销的领域：直播内容。
为了真正强调这一点，孔雀有一个“频道”部分，旨在模仿传统的线性电视。就其核心而言，孔雀就像一个重新想象的基本有线电视套装。在下面的截图中看到的频道，试图通过迎合不同的兴趣来使这一点变得更加明显。频道把孔雀装扮成有几个直播选项，但实际上孔雀并没有直播NBC环球有线频道。例如，NBC体育频道没有一直在播；但孔雀将有一个专门的频道来播放体育精彩节目，包括剪辑和其他与体育相关的节目。这种情况可能会随着2021年东京奥运会和2022年北京奥运会的到来而改变。
有几个直播新闻选项，但列出的大多数频道都是点播内容分组，孔雀似乎会过滤成一个线性的“直播”显示。点击“真正的犯罪”频道将播放“法医档案”的一集，但这不是现场娱乐节目。尽管如此，拥有一个下拉式频道指南感觉非常熟悉，对我来说也是一种安慰-这就是我们的目标。
NBC环球直接面向消费者的负责人吉登·卡茨(Gidon Katz)在这款应用发布前告诉The Verge，他们在过去三个月里了解到的是，人们想要感觉孔雀“活着”。频道可以重现基本的有线电视感觉，因为你可以在浏览时点击一个标题，然后让它开始播放，但它还没有完全到位。这些频道并不太令人兴奋，它们也不是字面意义上的生活。孔雀团队正寻求在未来几个月扩大其服务范围。
作为一项通用的流媒体服务，孔雀基本上是成功的。浏览部分或多或少和我们习惯的一样，而且已经足够了。如果孔雀的团队能想出如何处理Trending和Channels，孔雀成为特殊人物的机会肯定是存在的。目前，这款应用程序感觉功能很多，但实际上，我真正想从流媒体服务中得到的只是我的节目和电影，放在一个足够容易浏览的地方，而且内容设计周到。孔雀的浏览区做到了这一点；每当我使用孔雀时，我发现自己就呆在那个区域。
note_en:
Every new streaming service is looking for standout features to differentiate themselves from the competition, and  Peacock’s features try to combine three different types of experiences in one app: a traditional streaming interface, a curation of small, short-form newsy moments, and “live TV.” But after a few days with the Peacock app, what became very apparent to me is the app isn’t as robust as Comcast’s ambitions for it.
 Depending on  how people watch Peacock, they’ll be presented with two different viewing options. For people using Peacock on a TV, the app will automatically open up in “Browse,” the main streaming portal. A version of Browse being used on an iPhone XR can be seen in the left image below. For people using a mobile device or tablet, Peacock will open up in “Trending,” which acts as a collection of short news, entertainment, and sports clips. Think of it like a mixture of Instagram, YouTube, and Quibi all rolled into one. ( The Verge was given a preview version of the app, so some content was not available.)
 ( Disclosure: Comcast, which owns NBCUniversal, is also an investor in Vox Media, The Verge ’s parent company.)
  Let’s start with Browse, as many people are likely to stream Peacock via their TV sets, connected devices like PlayStation and Xbox consoles, or an Apple TV unit. The Browse section isn’t particularly inventive or impressive, but it works. Anyone who’s used Netflix or Hulu will already have a concrete understanding of how to use this particular section of the app. What it does accomplish, however, is showcasing NBCUniversal’s impressive catalog. It’s a treasure trove of IP laid out in an easy-to-navigate fashion that aggregates a ton of good TV in one place.
 Peacock’s main emphasis is on NBCUniversal’s catalog of titles. Considering that Peacock is launching without too many originals, having a library of beloved and in-demand titles is essential. Each row is designed to remind subscribers that NBCUniversal has a little bit of everything, from reality TV shows (including the  Real Housewives series and  Keeping up with the Kardashians) to acclaimed comedies ( 30 Rock,  Two and a Half Men,  Everybody Loves Raymond) to procedurals ( Law and Order: SVU,  Law and Order: Criminal Intent) and competition and home renovation shows.
 In that way, Peacock succeeds at reminding people why it should become an app they’ll open consistently. The first four tabs — Featured, TV Shows, Movies, and Kids — cater to that audience. It’s the fifth and sixth tabs within the browsing section that offer a little bit more insight into how Peacock differentiates itself from its competitors beyond content alone.
 Peacock has a dedicated news and sports tab and, most days, those tabs will be populated with live programming. On the news side, the top tile — that’s what Peacock’s team is calling the main slot at the top of the page — will likely change to NBC’s newly announced 24-hour Today Show programming,  Today All Day. For sports, a similar design will be used, but when it comes to high-profile exclusives like Premier League matches, those live events will have their own tile at the top of the Browse homepage with prominent promotion of the games.
  If Peacock’s vault of content is its biggest draw, why does the mobile version focus on its short clip “Trending” page? I couldn’t tell you. There isn’t anything substantial about the section. It doesn’t solve anything that NBCUniversal’s Twitter, Instagram, and YouTube pages don’t already do.
 The only reason I can see for having the section highlighted is because it feels like something that should be on a phone. The clips are short, and scrolling down through them is easy enough. NBCUniversal can also use the section to point Peacock subscribers toward what’s happening; for example, Premier League live games will be flagged as such within the Trending tab for people looking to jump into the game.
 Based on my time with the app, Trending isn’t an area I imagine people will use frequently. That said, the Peacock team is working on ways to expand its capabilities and offerings, and the team sees it as a way to help differentiate the service from its competitors. The Trending section also helps play into the area that Peacock really wants to sell subscribers on: live content.
 To really hammer home that point, Peacock has a “Channels” section that is designed to emulate traditional, linear TV. At its core, Peacock is like a reimagined basic cable package. Channels, seen in the screenshot below, try to make that more obvious by catering to different interests. Channels dress up Peacock to make it look like there are several live options, but Peacock doesn’t actually carry any live NBCUniversal cable channels. There’s no always-on NBC Sports, for example; but Peacock will have a dedicated channel for sports highlights that include clips and other sports-adjacent shows. That could change with the arrival of the Tokyo and Beijing Olympics in 2021 and 2022, respectively.
   There are a couple of live news options, but most of the channels listed are groupings of on-demand content that Peacock seems to filter into a linear, “live” display. Clicking on the True Crime channel will play an episode of  Forensic Files, but that isn’t live entertainment. Still, having a drop-down channel guide felt very familiar and was comforting to me — that’s the goal.
 Gidon Katz, head of direct to consumer at NBCUniversal,  told  The Verge  prior to the app’s launch that what they learned over the last three months is people wanted to feel like Peacock was “alive.” Channels can recreate that basic cable feeling because you can click on a title while browsing and just have it start playing, but it’s not quite there yet. The channels aren’t overly exciting, and they’re not live in the literal sense of the term. The Peacock team is looking to expand its offerings in the coming months.
 As a general streaming service, Peacock mostly succeeds. The Browsing section is more or less what we’re used to, and it’s more than enough. If Peacock’s team can figure out what to do with Trending and Channels, the opportunity for Peacock to be something special certainly exists. Right now, the app feels crowded with features that are only half-figured out when, in reality, all I really want from a streaming service is my shows and movies in one place that’s easy enough to browse through and with thoughtful content design. Peacock’s Browse section does that; and I find myself just staying in that area whenever I use Peacock.
title: 早期对孔雀的回顾：作为一项流媒体服务取得了成功，拥有熟悉的Browse部分和强大的库，但Trending和Channels部分并不令人印象深刻
title_en: Early review of Peacock: succeeds as a streaming service with familiar Browse section and a robust library, but Trending and Channels sections are underwhelming
